title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Democratic bills hit data center energy and water rules while more towns hit pause.

lines.text: Good morning, and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Today we're digging into new state bills aimed at data center power and water use, plus the wave of local pushback that's picking up speed.
That sounds like the story everyone's talking about right now.
Exactly. Yesterday Democratic senators introduced regulations focused on energy costs and water consumption for these big projects.
What exactly are they proposing, Graham?
The bills would set rules around how much power these facilities can pull and how they handle water use, especially in areas already feeling the strain.
And this comes right as local fights are heating up too?

Yes. In Kent County there's a recall petition drive targeting officials over a Microsoft proposal, and in Dowagiac a facility is shifting away from Bitcoin mining after noise complaints from neighbors.
Why do you think these bills are landing now?
It's the latest in a string of local actions. We've seen townships and cities across the state pausing or banning new projects to buy time for better rules.
How many places have done that already?
Quite a few. Lansing just approved a six-month freeze today. Haring Township residents turned out in big numbers yesterday pushing for an outright ban.
What are the main concerns driving all this?
Power demand that could raise electric bills for everyone, heavy water use that affects wells and ponds, noise that disrupts quiet neighborhoods, and land taken out of farmland or residential areas.
The water piece feels especially personal. We had that story about the pond draining during excavation on the Oracle and OpenAI campus.
Right. The water table bounced back after they finished digging, but it showed how quickly these projects can impact nearby homes and wetlands.
So the bills are trying to get ahead of that kind of issue statewide?
That's the idea. They want clearer standards on power and water before more projects lock in.
Who are the key players here?
Democratic senators are leading the legislative push. Locally you've got township boards, city councils, and citizen groups filing recalls or showing up at meetings.
What happens next with these bills?
They'll go through committee and hearings. In the meantime more local votes are coming up, like in Lowell Township tonight and Warren discussing a pause.
And places like Lyon Township already delayed their decision yesterday to give residents more time.
Exactly. The delay lets neighbors organize and ask questions about noise, traffic, and how much tax revenue actually stays local.
Speaking of taxes, there's the big Saline project.
The campus there is now valued at sixteen billion after financing closed. The township is deciding today on a one-hundred-forty-seven-million-dollar annual tax break.
Critics like the Attorney General are questioning why extra breaks are needed on top of state exemptions.
And the project is expected to create four hundred fifty permanent jobs, so supporters point to that economic upside.
How does that balance out for residents?
Bigger tax revenue potential but also bigger demands on power, roads, and services. Negotiations over abatements and upgrades are still happening.
Shifting to some of the other quick developments from yesterday.
Lowell Township residents are heading into their first data center vote tonight in Kent County, focused on noise, traffic, and what tax benefits they'll actually see.
And in Oakland County, Lyon Township postponed action again, leaving uncertainty over electricity demand and noise.
Warren's council is set to discuss its own temporary moratorium because of grid strain concerns in Macomb County.
Dowagiac residents got their first public look at plans on Monday, with questions about noise, water, and the electric grid.
And that facility there is already moving away from Bitcoin mining after complaints.
Today we also had the Lansing six-month freeze and the strong turnout in Haring Township for a full ban.
Hundreds showed up in Haring, signaling how deep the worry runs over losing rural character and facing higher utility bills.
It really feels like a pattern across the state now.
It does. More communities are hitting the brakes to figure out rules that protect residents while still allowing some development.
Let's look ahead to the meetings people can actually attend.
Today Saline Township has two special meetings on the property tax reduction and the industrial facilities tax exemption for the Oracle-linked project.
Tomorrow Ypsilanti Township has an informational session on the University of Michigan and Los Alamos proposal, including opposition updates and resource concerns.
That's a good chance for residents to ask questions before anything advances.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Senators Target Data Center Power and Water Use as Recalls and Noise Fights Escalate
